coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots promoted soybean photosynthesis and amino acid biosynthesis under drought stress: Reactive oxygen species scavenging and nitrogen metabolism

coredata.dc:description: With global warming and water scarcity, improving the drought tolerance and quality of crops is critical for food security and human health. Here, foliar application of carbon dots (CDs, 5 mg·L−1) could scavenge reactive oxygen species accumulation in soybean leaves under drought stress, thereby enhancing photosynthesis and carbohydrate transport. Moreover, CDs stimulated root secretion (e.g., amino acids, organic acids, and auxins) and recruited beneficial microorganisms (e.g., Actinobacteria, Ascomycota, Acidobacteria and Glomeromycota), which facilitate nitrogen (N) activation in the soil. Meanwhile, the expression of GmNRT, GmAMT, and GmAQP genes were up-regulated, indicating enhanced N and water uptake. The results demonstrated that CDs could promote nitrogen metabolism and enhance amino acid biosynthesis. Particularly, the N content in soybean shoots and roots increased significantly by 13.2 % and 30.5 %, respectively. The amino acids content in soybean shoots and roots increased by 257.5 % and 57.5 %, respectively. Consequently, soybean yields increased significantly by 21.5 %, and the protein content in soybean kernels improved by 3.7 %. Therefore, foliar application of CDs can support sustainable nano-enabled agriculture to combat climate change.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With global warming and water scarcity, improving the drought tolerance and quality of crops is critical for food security and human health. Here, foliar application of carbon dots (CDs, 5 mg·L−1) could scavenge reactive oxygen species accumulation in soybean leaves under drought stress, thereby enhancing photosynthesis and carbohydrate transport. Moreover, CDs stimulated root secretion (e.g., amino acids, organic acids, and auxins) and recruited beneficial microorganisms (e.g., Actinobacteria, Ascomycota, Acidobacteria and Glomeromycota), which facilitate nitrogen (N) activation in the soil. Meanwhile, the expression of GmNRT, GmAMT, and GmAQP genes were up-regulated, indicating enhanced N and water uptake. The results demonstrated that CDs could promote nitrogen metabolism and enhance amino acid biosynthesis. Particularly, the N content in soybean shoots and roots increased significantly by 13.2 % and 30.5 %, respectively. The amino acids content in soybean shoots and roots increased by 257.5 % and 57.5 %, respectively. Consequently, soybean yields increased significantly by 21.5 %, and the protein content in soybean kernels improved by 3.7 %. Therefore, foliar application of CDs can support sustainable nano-enabled agriculture to combat climate change.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past two decades, global warming, water scarcity and drought have remained major global environmental issues (Zhu et al., 2021). It is estimated that by 2030, at least 20 % of developing countries will face water scarcity (Chen et al., 2022a). Moreover, water shortage severely affects plant growth, and reduces crop yields by ∼25 % (Zou et al., 2021). Drought would induce the excess generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in plants, which causes serious oxidative damage to membranes and alters the function of photosynthesis, mineral absorption, and assimilation (Chen et al., 2022a). ROS, as one of the main indicators, can serve as a signaling molecule for plant defense system activation responding to environmental changes (Zhang et al., 2022). Therefore, how to scavenge ROS accumulation in leaves under abiotic stresses is crucial for alleviating its inhibition of photosynthesis and damage to other organelles. In recent years, the rise and advantages of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) have led to increased research and applications in agriculture (Gilbertson et al., 2020; Kah et al., 2019; Lowry et al., 2019). Metal oxide ENMs, such as CeO2 and ZnO ENMs, have excellent ROS scavenging activities, which can protect plant photosynthesis from oxidative stress and promote plant growth (Skiba et al., 2021). However, metal-based ENMs are expensive, unsuitable for general applications, and present environmental risks in long-term applications (Linh et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021b). Therefore, the affordable alternative ENMs with the role of scavenging ROS and promoting photosynthesis have attracted extensive attention.

Nitrogen (N) is an essential nutrient element in plant growth and development (Gautrat et al., 2021). It not only promotes the synthesis of chlorophyll to enhance photosynthesis, but also is an important component of plant cells, which is inseparable from the growth and resistance of plants (Chen et al., 2020; Song et al., 2019). As a fundamental metabolic process in plants, N metabolism is also a key pathway for amino acids and protein biosynthesis (Li et al., 2018b; Ru et al., 2022). The enhancement of N metabolism can significantly improve the stress resistance of plants (Han et al., 2020). Therefore, the improvement of N use efficiency (NUE), especially under drought stress, is important for improving the drought tolerance and quality of crops (Baslam et al., 2020). Currently, the main measures to promote N metabolism in crops are to increase the supply of N fertilizer or to improve the N uptake and utilization by crops (Congreves et al., 2021; Fiaz et al., 2021; Zulfiqar et al., 2019). However, the over-application of N fertilizers did not lead to a sustained increase in crop yields. According to FAO statistics, the total global N fertilizer consumption has reached 193.5 million tons in 2020, but the NUE is lower than 50 %. Excessive application of N fertilizers has caused serious environmental problems such as soil acidification and water eutrophication (Zulfiqar et al., 2019). Recent studies have shown that soil application of multi-walled carbon nanotubes can effectively promote nitrogen metabolism in Brassica campestris, contributing to an 85.9 % increase in NUE (Cai et al., 2022). Therefore, improving N uptake and metabolism by plants is an effective way to improve NUE, which is of great significance for improving crop quality, reducing nitrogen fertilizer consumption, and alleviating environmental pollution (Anas et al., 2020).

As a member of the nanocarbon families (e.g., multi-walled carbon nanotubes, single-walled carbon nanotubes, carbon black and graphene nanoplatelets), carbon dots (CDs) have the advantages of small size, unique optical properties, and good biocompatibility, which show great application prospects in biomedical, agriculture and other fields (Bhati et al., 2018; Dubey et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020a; Sonkar et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2022a). Especially in the past three years, CDs-enabled agriculture has developed rapidly, showing great potential for promoting photosynthesis, growth and stress resistance of crops (Li et al., 2021b; Li et al., 2022). For instance, soil application of CDs (5 mg·kg−1) enhanced soybeans growth under drought stress, which was better than that of other carbon nanomaterials (Wang et al., 2022a). Soil application of CDs (3 mg·kg−1) could modulate the antioxidant enzyme activities, eliminate ROS accumulation, and promote tomato growth under drought stress (Chen et al., 2022a). Foliar CDs (5 mg·L−1) amendment can modulate carbohydrate metabolism, rhizospheric properties, and increase N bioavailability in maize (Yang et al., 2022). However, it is unclear whether CD can enhance the drought tolerance and quality of crops by eliminating ROS and improving N bioavailability. Therefore, we hypothesized that foliar application of CDs under drought stress could protect photosynthesis by eliminating ROS accumulation, and promote the N uptake and metabolism thereby improving the drought tolerance and quality of crops. As a globally important economic crop, the annual production of soybean (Glycine max) has reached 366 million tons. However, the drought may reduce soybean yields by 25 % ∼ 50 % annually (Zou et al., 2021). Herein, soybean was chosen as a model plant to investigate: (1) the roles of CDs in enhancing photosynthesis under drought stress; (2) whether CDs could regulate soybean root exudates and soil microorganisms; (3) the specific mechanism by which CDs enhance N uptake and metabolism; (4) the effects of CDs on soybean growth, yield and quality.

CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method according to previous research (Wang et al., 2021b). Citric acid (1 g, Sigma Aldrich) and ethylenediamine (335 μL, Sigma Aldrich) were dissolved in ultrapure water (10 mL) and injected into an autoclave chamber, then heated at 200 °C for 12 h. Finally, the solid CDs were obtained by freeze-drying. The shape and size of CDs were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM–2100, Hitachi, Japan), and the particle size distribution was analyzed by measurement software (Nano measurer 1.2). Fluorescence spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F–7000, Hitachi, Japan), and X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained by X–ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Kalpha, US). The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR, Tensor 27, Bruker Optics, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Soybean cultivation and foliar application of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Soil was collected from Wuxi, China (31°29′N, 120°20′E), and soil parameters were analyzed before soybean planting, and the results are listed in Table S1. Soybean seeds (Gmax_ZH13) were purchased from the Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences. Seeds were sterilized with 5 % H2O2 solution for 10 min and then rinsed repeatedly with deionized water. After soaking for 8 h, the sterile seeds were incubated in the dark for 3 days at 25 °C with an average humidity of 60 %. After germination, three uniform seedlings were individually transplanted into each pot (1.5 gallon-pot with 2 kg soil) and grown in natural light (25 °C, humidity ∼50 %). The pots were randomly arranged with six replications for each treatment group, and the soil moisture (SM) was maintained at 75 % for 25 days. For the next 5 days, drought conditions began to be created. On the 30th day, the pots were divided into three groups: CK (non–drought-stress, 75 % SM), DS (drought-stress, 35 % SM), and DS + M (drought-stress treated with CDs, 35 % SM). In DS + M treatment, soybean leaves were sprayed with CDs solution (5 mg·L−1, 5 ml per plant) once every day for one week. While in DS treatment, ultrapure water was sprayed on soybean leaves as control. The soil moisture sensor (TZS–IW, China) was used to measure soil moisture content in all pots. On the 38th day, a portion of soybean seedlings were harvested. Soybean shoots, roots, and nodules were immediately gently frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C until further analysis. Furthermore, the remaining soybeans were grown to maturity in the same manner and harvested on day 120. Soybean shoots, roots, pods, and kernels were preserved in the same way as at the seedling stage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of soybean growth and photosynthetic parameters
ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthesis parameters of soybean leaves were determined using a CIRAS–3 portable gas exchange system (Hansatech, USA). Chlorophyll fluorescence kinetic parameters were analyzed by a plant efficiency analyzer (Pocket PEA, Hansatech Instruments Ltd., UK). Chlorophyll content (Li et al., 2018a), carbohydrate content (Yang et al., 2022), and MDA (Malondialdehyde) content (Zhang et al., 2013) of soybeans were measured according to previous reports. The specific experimental methods are shown in text S1. The detection of ROS accumulation in chloroplasts was observed by a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Nikon, Japan) under 488 nm excitation, as detailed in text S2. Root parameters (root length, surface area, average diameter, and number of tips) were analyzed using a root scanner (WinRHIZO Pro 2017b, Canada). At harvest, the fresh weight (FW) of soybean shoot, roots, nodules, and kernels were recorded immediately. Subsequently, soybean shoots, roots and kernels were dried at 105 °C for 15 min, then dried to constant weight in an oven at 75 °C to record dry weight (DW). The relative moisture content of soybeans was calculated according to a previous report (Chen et al., 2022a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of soybean root exudates and soil microbial communities
ce:section.ce:para: To collect the root exudates of soybean, the harvested soybean roots were immediately washed several times with ultrapure water (conductivity ≥ 18.25 MΩ·cm), and then transferred to a flask containing 100 mL of ultrapure water. The ultrapure water in the flask was collected and concentrated with 20 folds, then the concentrate was filtered through a 22 μm filter membrane. Subsequently, samples were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS, APS80–16PLUS, Thermo Scientific, Germany) according to a previous method (Yue et al., 2022). More details on the measurement of root exudates are shown in text S3.

At harvest, soybean rhizosphere soil was collected for determination of soil microorganisms. Soil bacterial and fungal community compositions were analyzed by high-throughput sequencing technique, which was carried out by an Illumina HiSeq 2500 instrument at Personalbio Company (Shanghai, China). The primers used for sequencing were 338F (5′-ACTCC TACGG GAGGC AGCAG-3′) and 806R (5′-GGACT ACHVG GGTWT CTAAT-3′). After sequencing the V3–V4 and ITS regions, the original readings (original PE) were obtained and low-quality fragments were filtered to recover effective tags.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enzymatic activity determination and analysis of nitrogen metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: For N content determination in soybean shoots and roots, 5 mg dry samples were packaged according to the manufacturer's instructions and analyzed using an automated elemental analyzer (UNICUBE, Elementar, Germany).

The content of NO3 −, NH4 +, nitrate reductase (NR), and nitrite reductase (NiR) activities in soybean shoots and roots was determined and analyzed using assay kits (Suzhou Grace Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). Glutamine synthase (GS), glutamate synthase (GOGAT), glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH), aspartate aminotransferase (AAT) and aspartate aminotransferase (ASN) activities in soybean kernels were determined using assay kits (Suzhou Grace Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). All reagents, samples, and standards were prepared according to the manufacturer's protocol, and the calculation steps of the both results were carried out by following the instructions.

Total RNA from soybean shoots and roots was extracted using MiniBEST Plant RNA Extraction Kit (Takara, Japan), then mixed with EasyQuick RT MasterMix (Kangwei, China), and reverse transcribed into cDNA using an ultra-micro spectrophotometer (UltraM–QB200, Gallop, China). The expression levels of GmSUTs, GmNRTs, GmAMTs, and GmAQPs were quantified with Ultra SYBR Mixture (CWBIO) using a CFX96TM Optics Module real-time PCR detection system (Bio-Rad, USA). The specific primer sequences were listed in Table S2. The thermal program for qRT–PCR amplification was 95 °C for 30 s, followed by 40 cycles of 95 °C for 15 s and 60 °C for 30 s, with a melting curve of 65 to 95 °C. Six replicates were performed for each biological sample. Ultimately, the relative gene expression was calculated using the 2−ΔΔCT method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination and analysis of soybean kernels
ce:section.ce:para: The amino acids content in soybean kernels was determined and analyzed by LC–MS/MS (Vanquish Flex, Thermofisher Scientific, Germany following our previous study (Yue et al., 2022). LC–MS/MS analyses were performed using a UHPLC system (Vanquish Flex, Thermofisher Scientific, Germany), with a HILIC column (2.1 × 100 mm, 1.8 μm) and a Q-Exactive Plus mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The detailed extraction of soybean kernels, and the specific setup and analysis of LC–MS/MS are shown in text S4. The protein content of soybean kernels was determined by Kjeldahl automatic N analyzer (K9840, Hanon, China).

The results of the measured parameters were expressed as the mean ± standard deviation of at least three biological replicates. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with the Fisher LSD test was used to analyze the variation among different treatments using Origin Statistics 2019b at p < 0.05. For root exudates, all data analyses were based on MetaboAnalyst 5.0 (https://www.metaboanalyst.ca/). For 16S rRNA and ITS sequencing of microorganisms, all data were conducted using QIIME 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs eliminated ROS accumulation and protected photosystem
ce:section.ce:para: Prepared CDs showed a size distribution of 1–8 nm (Fig. S1a) with a uniform spherical shape, and the average size of CDs was 3.8 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. S1b). The CDs had blue fluorescence at 365 nm excitation (Fig. S1c). The FT–IR results confirmed the existence of hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CDs (Fig. S1d) (Li et al., 2022; Li et al., 2021c). The XPS spectra indicated that CDs consisted of C, N, and O (Fig. S2a), and the high-resolution C 1s XPS spectra confirmed the presence of CC, CN, CO, and CO in CDs (Fig. S2b) (Yao et al., 2022). The high-resolution O 1s XPS spectra was divided into three-unit moieties: CO, CO, and –OH (Fig. S2c) (Yao et al., 2022). Furthermore, the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectra confirmed the presence of pyrrolic N, pyridinic N and graphitic N (Fig. S2d) (Li et al., 2021a).

Previous studies have shown that abiotic stresses (e.g., drought and salinity) can lead to an increase in ROS, seriously impairing the normal growth of plants (Liu et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022). However, CDs had been shown to be transported into the plant tissues and have ability to scavenge intracellular ROS (Luo et al., 2021). CDs could scavenge intracellular ROS, and effectively alleviate oxidative damage in plants due to their small size and physicochemical structure (Li et al., 2020a; Li et al., 2020b). Chen et al. also demonstrated that CDs could regulate enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant systems, scavenge ROS, and inhibit lipid peroxidation, thereby protecting the membrane structure and function of plant cells under stress (Chen et al., 2022a). Herein, TEM images illustrated that CDs could penetrate the mesophyll cell wall and plasma membrane of soybean leaves, thus dispersing around the chloroplast (Fig. 1a and Fig. S3). In addition, CLSM images indicated that drought stress apparently induced the accumulation of ROS in soybean leaves, while CDs effectively scavenged the ROS accumulated in soybean leaves (Fig. 1b).

High levels of ROS can cause lipid peroxidation, protein denaturation, or DNA damage, which adversely affect cells. For example, drought can lead to elevated MDA content. MDA is the final decomposition product of membrane lipid peroxidation, and may cause certain damage to membranes and cells (Zou et al., 2021). In this study, compared with DS (drought stress), the MDA content in soybean leaves was decreased by 43.6 % (Fig. 2a) and the chlorophyll content was increased by 7.3 % (Fig. 2b) after CDs application. Therefore, CDs could alleviate the oxidative damage in soybean leaves scavenge excess ROS and protect the photosystem.

In addition, maximum photon quantum efficiency (Fv/Fm), fluorescence decay rate (Rfd), and non-photochemical quenching (NPQ) indicated that soybean leaves were severely damaged under drought stress, but would recover after CDs application (Fig. 2c). Fv/Fm and Rfd showed fluorescence after dark adaptation and light adaptation, respectively, and specific bands from dark blue to red indicated mild to severe leaf damage. NPQ exhibited fluorescence under light, with specific bands ranging from red to dark blue indicating mild to severe leaf damage. Furthermore, fluorescence parameters such as qL, qN, and qP were significantly decreased under drought stress, whereas all of these parameters were enhanced after CDs application (Fig. 2d and Table S3). These results indicated that although soybean growth was significantly better under drought stress after applying CDs, it was still inferior to CK. Overall, CDs can enter plant cells, disperse around chloroplasts, and reduce ROS accumulation under drought stress. Such actions are beneficial to protect the photosystem of soybean leaves and effectively promote photosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs promoted soybean growth and water uptake
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with DS, foliar application of CDs significantly increased net photosynthesis rate (Pn) by 32.6 % (Fig. 3a). Additionally, stomatal conductance (Gs), intracellular CO2 concentrations (Ci), and transpiration rate (Tr) were also improved by 23.1 %, 16.9 % and 26.6 %, respectively (Fig. S4a–S4c). These results suggested that CDs could promote photosynthesis due to the elimination of ROS accumulation and the enhancement of chlorophyll content. Photosynthesis is an essential metabolic process that provides necessary nutrients for plant growth and development (Li et al., 2021b). Previous studies demonstrated that ENMs could modulate photosynthesis to improve plant growth (Liu et al., 2020c; Shang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2013). In this study, soybeans grew best under CK, and soybeans grew significantly better under drought stress after CDs were applied (Fig. 3b). After CDs application, the FW of soybean shoots and roots increased by 17.4 % and 31.5 % (Fig. 3c), and the DW of soybean shoots and roots improved by 8.7 % and 10.6 %, respectively (Fig. 3d). In addition, the growth of soybean roots was also significantly superior to DS after CDs application (Fig. S5a). For instance, the root length, root average diameter, root surface area and root tips increased by 24.7 %, 17.0 %, 27.6 %, and 22.3 %, respectively (Fig. S5b–S5e). These results implied that CDs could ensure the survival and growth of soybeans under drought stress.

As the crucial photosynthetic products, carbohydrates provide the carbon framework and energy for plant growth and development (Yang et al., 2022). Sucrose, the main component of carbohydrates, can be transported from source leaves to sink organs. The family of SUTs (sucrose transporters) and SWEETs (sugars will eventually be exported transporters) play key roles in carbohydrate transport (Chen et al., 2022b). Compared with DS, the carbohydrate content in soybean shoots and roots increased by 35.4 % and 31.8 %, respectively, after CDs application (Fig. 3e). Furthermore, the expression of GmSUT2, GmSweet6, and GmSweet15 genes were up-regulated by 23.3 %, 49.1 %, and 33.7 % respectively, in soybean shoots (Fig. 3f), and 174.6 %, 119.2 %, and 43.0 % (Fig. 3g), respectively, in soybean roots. Therefore, foliar application of CDs could promote carbohydrate accumulation and transport in soybean shoots and roots due to enhanced photosynthesis.

Moreover, the application of CDs under drought stress can promote water uptake by soybeans. The moisture content of soybean shoots and roots increased by 1.4 % and 4.4 %, respectively (Fig. 3h). The relative expression of GmPIP1;7, GmPIP1;10, GmPIP2;4, and GmPIP2;6 genes were up-regulated by 2.2, 1.4, 3.9, and 4.8-fold, respectively. Additionally, the expression of GmTIP1;7 and GmTIP1;8 genes were increased by 1.9 and 1.6-fold, respectively (Fig. 3i). Aquaporins (AQPs) are a diverse family of membrane channel proteins that are extensively involved in plant physiological processes and play crucial regulatory roles under various environmental stresses (Kapilan et al., 2018). For example, studies reported that AQPs could serve as potential regulators of plant cell water relations (Feng et al., 2019). The opening of AQPs can promote plant roots to absorb more water and enhance drought tolerance (Wang et al., 2022a). Therefore, the above results suggested that the application of CDs ensured the growth of soybean under drought stress, promoted the transport of carbohydrates, and stimulated the uptake of water by the roots, ultimately enhancing the drought tolerance and moisture content of soybean.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs increased root exudates and rhizosphere microbes of soybean
ce:section.ce:para: The principal component analysis (PCA) revealed a clear separation (PC1: 64.9 %) of CK, DS, and DS+M, indicating that CDs significantly altered metabolite profile (Fig. S6a). Based on the univariate statistical analyses (Student's t-test, p < 0.05, or VIP score > 1.5), 49 metabolites with significant variations were screened, and they were classified into: amino acids (26.5 %), organic acids (24.5 %), auxin (14.3 %), fatty acids (6.1 %), nucleotides (4.1 %), and others (24.5 %) (Fig. S6b). These results showed that the application of CDs under drought stress improved the secretion of soybean roots (Fig. 4a), with significant correlations of soybean root exudates among the different treatments (Table S4). Specifically, the significantly up-regulated metabolites included glycitein, coumestrol, soyasaponin I, indole, and asiatic acid, belonging to amino acids, auxins, and organic acids (Fig. S6c), most of which were beneficial for plant growth (Zhang et al., 2019). Therefore, foliar application of CDs is beneficial in promoting root exudation of soybean under drought stress because of the enhancement of photosynthesis and carbohydrate transport.

Given that plant root metabolites can serve as carbon sources for soil microbes, changes in root exudates may alter soil microbial communities (Sasse et al., 2018; Tian et al., 2020). Approximately 16,790 observed bacterial community OTUs were generated, of which 3.50 % OTUs were shared by the three groups (Fig. S7a); 1535 observed fungal community OTUs were yielded, and 4.69 % OTUs were shared by the three groups (Fig. S7c). The PCA plots (PC1 of bacteria: 80 %, PC1 of fungi: 59.1 %) indicated that there were significant differences in both bacteria and fungi communities (Fig. S7b and S7d). As shown in Fig. 4b, Proteobacteria were the most dominant bacteria at the phylum level, followed by Bacteroidetes, Actinobacteria, Acidobacteria, Gemmatimonadetes, and Cyanobacteria. The predominant fungal phyla were Ascomycota, Basidiomycota, and Glomeromycota. Apparently, the abundance of microorganisms decreased under drought stress. However, compared with DS, the relative abundance of major microorganisms increased by 37.3 % after CDs application (Fig. 4b and Table S5), which was positively correlated with the alteration of root exudates.

Organic acids will acidify the soil environment and activate or transform insoluble nutrients (such as N and P) in the soil through exchange and reduction (Zhao et al., 2019). Auxin play a key role in regulating rhizosphere ecological processes and enhancing the absorption of rhizosphere nutrients by plants (Gargallo-Garriga et al., 2018). Flavonoids can promote the symbiotic N fixation between plants and microorganisms by mobilizing or absorbing mineral nutrients (Yu et al., 2021). Moreover, the increase of microorganisms is also beneficial for the activation of N in the soil. Previous studies suggested that Actinobacteria could promote the conversion of N in soil by enhancing the decomposition or formation of humus (Zhang et al., 2019). Bacteroidetes and Acidobacteria are functional bacteria that contribute to C and N cycling in the soil (Kelly et al., 2022). Therefore, the application of CDs promoted the N activation in the soil by increasing root exudates and enriching beneficial microorganisms in the rhizosphere, which in turn enhanced N uptake by soybeans.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs enhanced N uptake and metabolism
ce:section.ce:para: Legume crops such as soybean can obtain N from soil or air mainly through root nodule symbiosis, which converts N into NH4 + that can be used by plants (Wang et al., 2022b). In this study, the number of nodules per plant increased by 18.1 % compared with DS (Fig. S8a). Hence, CDs indirectly promoted the growth of soybean nodules and facilitated N2 fixation in soybeans due to the mitigation of drought stress and the activation of N in the soil. The main forms of N uptake by soybean are NO3 − and NH4 +, which are mediated by the nitrate nitrogen transport (NRT) family and the ammonium nitrogen transport (AMT) family, respectively (Chiasson et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2020). Previous studies showed that the enhanced activity of NRT and AMT transporters contributes to the promotion of N uptake and transport in plants (Kobae et al., 2010; Peng et al., 2019). After application of CDs under drought stress, the expression of GmNRT2;1, GmNRT2;2, GmNRT2;4, GmNRT2;5, and GmNRT2;6 genes were up-regulated by 2.6, 3.1, 1.5, 2.8, and 1.3-fold, respectively, (Fig. S8b), and the expression of GmAMT1.4, GmAMT3.1, GmAMT4.1, GmAMT4.3, and GmAMT4.4 genes were improved by 1.4, 2.9, 1.8, 2.0, and 1.6-fold, respectively (Fig. S8c). These results indicated that the increase of nodules and the growth of soybean significantly promoted the expression of N transporters, thereby enhancing the uptake and transport of N (Fig. 5 ). As a result, the N content in soybean shoots and roots improved by 13.2 % and 30.5 %, respectively (Fig. S8d).

Previous studies found that N assimilation and utilization occurred both in the shoots and roots of plants (Gautrat et al., 2021). NH4 + can be directly used for the synthesis of amino acids, while NO3 − needs to be reduced to NH4 + by nitrate reductase (NR) and nitrite reductase (NiR) after entering cells, and then participate in the metabolism of amino acids. NR and NiR are key enzymes in N metabolism that regulate the assimilation of primary N (Wang et al., 2020). In this study, NR activities in soybean shoots and roots increased by 3.9 % and 2.9 %, respectively (Fig. S9a); and NiR activities in soybean shoots and roots increased by 26.8 % and 34.7 %, respectively (Fig. S9b). Consequently, the NO3 − contents in soybean shoots and roots increased by 8.5 % and 10.1 % (Fig. S9c), and the NH4 + contents in soybean shoots and roots improved by 11.6 % and 5.8 %, respectively (Fig. S9d). These results demonstrated that CDs increased the contents of NH4 + and NO3 − in soybean by enhancing the nitrate reductase (NR) and nitrite reductase (NiR) activities, which were beneficial in promoting the synthesis of amino acids (Fig. 5). Meanwhile, total amino acids contents in soybean shoots and roots increased by 257.5 % and 57.5 %, respectively (Fig. S10a). Overall, CDS effectively promotes the absorption and metabolism of nitrogen by improving the photosynthesis and activating the N in the soil, which is conducive to the biological synthesis of amino acids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs improved amino acid biosynthesis in soybean kernels
ce:section.ce:para: The above results confirmed that CDs enhanced N uptake and metabolism in soybeans. Plants assimilate inorganic N (mainly NH4 + and NO3 −) from the environment into organic N compounds, such as amino acids (Liu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2021a). Herein, glutamate, glutamine, arginine, proline, and histidine were improved by 6.6 %, 10.8 %, 16.5 %, 23.5 %, and 30.2 %, respectively (Fig. 6 and Table S6), which were regulated by N metabolism. Among them, glutamic acid and aspartic acid are the two main amino acids that can be further synthesized for other amino acids (Asakura et al., 2021). Glutamate is derived from 2-oxoglutarate and is used by glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) to catalyze the synthesis of other amino acids. NH4 + synthesizes other amino acids through the glutamate-glutamine (GS-GOGAT) cycle, in which glutamine synthase (GS) and glutamate synthase (GOGAT) play important roles (Hu et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018b). GS, GOGAT, and GDH enzymes are essential in NH4 + assimilation and metabolism, and are jointly involved in glutamate synthesis. In this study, CDs enhanced the enzymatic activities of GS, GOGAT, and GDH by 8.3 %, 6.3 %, and 36.1 %, respectively (Fig. S10b-S10d). Furthermore, the contents of aspartate, asparagine, lysine, methionine, threonine, and isoleucine were increased by 17.4 %, 26.4 %, 43.0 %, 20.6 %, 21.2 %, and 15.5 %, respectively (Fig. 6 and Table S6). In the TCA cycle, oxaloacetate is first converted to aspartate, which is then catalyzed by aspartate aminotransferase (AAT) to aspartate, and then catalyzed by asparagine synthase (ASN) to asparagine (Liu et al., 2020b). Under drought stress, CDs enhanced the enzymatic activities of AAT and ASN were increased by 24.4 % and 76.0 %, respectively (Fig. S10e, S10f). Therefore, the improvement of photosynthesis and N uptake is beneficial to promote the synthesis of amino acids, especially glutamic acid and aspartic acid.

Overall, CDs significantly promoted amino acids biosynthesis in soybean kernels, with a 16.9 % increase in total amino acids content (Fig. S11). Hence, we infer that the improvement in photosynthesis could fix more CO2, which in turn produces more glucose for glycolysis and facilitates the TCA cycle. Meanwhile, the enhancement of N absorption and metabolism further accelerated the biosynthesis of amino acids, which contributed to the improvement of crop stress tolerance and quality. In Fig. S12a, the growth of soybean pods and kernels was obviously inhibited under drought stress, but recovered after CDs application. The number of soybean pods and kernels per plant increased by 9.2 % and 26.4 %, respectively, (Fig. S12b and S12c), the yield and 100-grain weight improved by 21.5 % and 7.1 %, respectively (Fig. S12d and S12e). Besides, amino acids could further synthesize proteins and play a crucial role in the primary and secondary metabolism of plants. The protein content in soybean kernels increased by 3.7 % after CDs application under drought stress (Fig. S13). In summary, foliar application of CDs significantly improved soybean yield and quality by enhancing photosynthesis and amino acid biosynthesis.

Foliar spraying of CDs (5 mg L−1) significantly improved photosynthesis and N metabolism in soybean under drought stress. With the aggravation of environmental problems such as greenhouse effect and water shortage, the improvement of soybean drought tolerance is beneficial to ensure the yield and quality under drought conditions. On the one hand, CDs enhanced photosynthesis by alleviating ROS accumulation, which in turn promoted carbohydrate accumulation and transport, stimulated root secretion, and recruited beneficial microorganisms. CDs not only enhanced water uptake in soybean under drought stress, but also indirectly activated N in the soil. On the other hand, CDs enhanced N uptake and transport in soybean and promoted amino acid biosynthesis. Finally, the amino acids and protein content in soybean kernels were increased. Taken together, CDs have the potential to enhance soybean drought tolerance and nitrogen use efficiency (NUE), which provides a new perspective for improving grain yield and quality and ensuring sustainable agricultural development.
